english: goods or money, gós-skyin (gos skyin) of clothes. — skyín-mí (skyin mi) Schr. debtor. — skyin-tsáb (skyin tshab) C.: the pledge for a loan; acc. to others, how-ever, it just means the object lent or its equivalent when being returned.

everything giving shelter from above, an overhanging rock, a roof etc.: *čar skyib* (*char skyib*) shelter from rain; *ḍag-skyib* (*brag skyib*) under a ṕa-boṅ (pha bong) q. v. (gyam (gyam) is much larger, pủg-pa (phug pa) deeper) W.; bka-sky. (bka' skyibs) a covered terrace or small portico before a house.

pf. & ft. bskyil (bskyil) 1. to bend, esp. the legs when sitting on the ground after Oriental fashion, also another's leg by a kick from behind; to bend the bow. — 2. to pen up, shut up, cattle, to dam up, a river, also: ču rdziṅ-du skyil-ba (chu rdzing du skyil ba) to collect water into a pond Glr., or rdziṅ-bu sky. (rdzing bu skyil); to dam up a pond (but not 'to dig it' Schr.); to keep back, retain, detain a person W.; *k̉a kyíl-če* (*kha skyil ce*) to keep a person from doing something, to dissuade from W. — skyil-krúṅ (skyil krung), also skyil-mo-krúṅ (skyil mo krung), the posture of sitting cross-legged, skyil-krúṅ byéd-pa (skyil krung byed pa) (resp. mdzád-pa (mdzad pa)), skyil-mo-krúṅ-gis (skyil mo krung gis) (or du (du)) dúg-pa ('dug pa) (resp. bźúgs-pa (bzhugs pa)) to assume such a posture; séms-dpai skyil-krúṅ (sems dpa'i skyil krung) the usual manner of sitting, in which the feet are not seen, rdo-rjei sky. (rdo rje'i skyil krung) the posture in which the soles of the feet are seen turned upwards, rdzógs-pai sky. (rdzogs pa'i skyil krung) another posture requiring particular practice. (The spelling dkyil-krúṅ (dkyil krung), though frequent, is expressly rejected by grammarians.) — *skyíl-díṅ* (*skyil ding*) W. a small hole filled with water. — *skil-ldír* (*skil ldir*) W. handle, ring fixed to a thing, for carrying it, hanging it up etc.
Lex. w.e., Sch.: a gulp, draught.

a sour fruit Med.; skyú-ru-ra (skyu ru ra) Med. (Lex.: wood - sorrel) the same (?); in later times the word seems to have been used also for the olive, and skyú-ru-šiṅ (skyu ru shing) the olive tree, which in Sik. is called ḱa-skyúr-poi šiṅ (kha skyur po'i shing).
Cs.: 'condiment, sauce, pickle', acc. to others, at least in W., only the resp. word for spags (spags): 1. sauce, gravy. 2. dish, mess.

pf. skyugs (skyugs). 1. to vomit, eject, e.g. blood, skyúg-tu ǰúg-pa (skyug tu 'jug pa) to cause to vomit, skyúg-pa drêṅ-pa (skyug pa 'dren pa) to excite vomiting Tar.; skyúgs-pa (skyugs pa) (partic. pf.), ṅan-skyúgs (ngan skyugs), the vomit (it is the food of certain demons, and being boiled in it, is one of the punishments of hell). — 2 to lose colour, to stain. Comp. skyug-ldád (skyug ldad) rumination, chewing the cud; Sch. also: eructation. — skyúg-bro-ba (skyug bro ba) nausea, skyúg-bro-bai nad (skyug bro ba'i nad) disease of nausea; skyúg-bro-bas (skyug bro bas) from disgust; skyug-bro (skyug bro) C. also impure with regard to religion, = W. *tsid-du* (*tsid du*). — skyug-smán (skyug sman) an emetic. — skyúg-log-pa (skyug log pa) Sch. to feel disgust.
also lčúṅ-ka (lcung ka), jack-daw (black, with a red bill); skyúṅ-kas zos (skyung kas zos) Lex. eaten or stolen by a jack-daw.
pf. bskyuṅs (bskyungs), ft. bskyuṅ (bskyung), imp. skyuṅ(s) (skyung(s)) Cs. to leave behind, to lay aside, e.g. a task Lex., pride S.g.
1. Cs.: to forget, leave off. 2. Sch.: to comminute; to swallow. (?)

I. adj. sour, sbst. acidity; more frq.: skyúr-po (skyur po) C., -mo (-mo) W. adj. sour; skyur ǰúg-pa (skyur 'jug pa) 1. to turn sour. 2. to suffer a substance to turn sour, v. ǰug-pa ('jug pa). — ḱa(-ša)-skyúr-po (kha (sha) skyur po) olive, ḱa(ša)-skyur-pói šiṅ (kha (sha) skyur po'i shing) olive tree Sik. — skyúr-ku (skyur khu) Cs., raṅ-skyúr (rang skyur) Cs., skyúr-ru (skyur ru) (Sik.), skúr-mo (skur mo) Lh. a sour liquid, vinegar. (Vinegar seems to be little known as yet in Tibet, and the above mentioned expressions may have been framed by different persons on different occasions, but are not in general use. The same may be said of Cs.’s skyúr-pa (skyur pa) and skyur-rtsí (skyur rtsi) for acid in a chemical sense.) II. vb. pf. & ft. bskyur (bskyur) 1. to throw, to cast, ǰyír (phyir) out, lhuṅ-zéd nám-mk̉a-la bskyur-nas (lhung bzed nam mkha' la bskyur nas) having flung his mendicant's-bowl up into the air Dzl., čur skyúr-ba (chur skyur ba) to throw into the water, rgyáb-tu (rgyab tu) behind one's self = to
